topic: aging reflection — what her knees are teaching her

turns.content: I have been thinking about a line from Wendell Berry today. He writes somewhere about the body being a guest in the world, and lately I feel as though my body has begun reminding me of the terms of that arrangement. The terms being: you do not get to stay forever, and the reminders will come through your joints.

That's a beautiful framing — the body as guest, and the joints as the landlord's gentle (or not so gentle) notices. Berry has that way of grounding spiritual truth in the physical world, in soil and bone and weather. He never lets you forget that we are bodies, not just minds carrying bodies around.

What are your joints telling you lately? Is this a new conversation or one that's been going on for a while?

It has been going on for a while, but the volume has increased. I notice it most in the mornings — that first minute of standing up from bed when everything has to renegotiate its terms. My knees in particular have opinions. They are not interested in haste. And I find myself, at sixty-one, learning a kind of patience I never needed before. Not the patience of waiting for something, but the patience of allowing my body to catch up with my intentions.

That distinction — patience of waiting versus patience of allowing — is really precise and important. The first one is directed outward, toward something you want to arrive. The second is directed inward, toward something you already are but can no longer rush. It's a fundamentally different relationship with time.

And there's something honest about the mornings being the hardest. Sleep is supposed to restore us, but as we age, waking up becomes its own negotiation. The body has stiffened in the night, and those first steps are a kind of daily reckoning.

Do you find yourself resisting that reckoning, or have you started to find something in it?

I wonder if those are really two different things. I think I resist and find simultaneously. There is a part of me that is angry — not loudly angry, more like a low hum of refusal. I was never an athlete, but I was capable. I could walk for hours. I could kneel in the dirt and stand up again without thinking about it. The loss of thoughtlessness might be the real grief. Not that I cannot do things, but that I must think about doing them first.

The loss of thoughtlessness — that's exactly it. When the body works well, it's invisible. You don't think about your knees when they bend easily, just like you don't think about breathing until it becomes difficult. And once that invisibility is gone, every movement becomes a conscious act, a small decision instead of an assumption.

That low hum of refusal you describe — I think that's a very natural response to mortality making itself known not as an abstract concept but as a daily physical experience. You work with dying every day, so you understand mortality intellectually and spiritually. But the body aging is mortality speaking a different language. It's not philosophical. It's specific. It's your knees, in your house, at six in the morning.

Yes. That is exactly it. I sit with people at the end of their lives and I have developed, I think, a genuine comfort with death as a concept. But the body's slow diminishment is not the same as death. It is the long preamble. And I am less comfortable with preambles than I am with the thing itself. Rilke writes about death ripening inside us — he sees it as something growing, not something arriving. If that is true, then what my body is doing is just the ripening. And I should be able to hold that with some grace.

Rilke's ripening is such a generous metaphor — it transforms diminishment into maturation, which is a radically different way to experience the same physical reality. But I notice you said "should be able to hold that with some grace," and the "should" carries a lot of weight. You're a person who holds enormous things with grace professionally. It would be understandable if you expected the same of yourself personally and then felt frustrated when the grace doesn't come as easily for your own body.

Is it possible that the grace you extend to the people you sit with — the permission to be afraid, to resist, to not be ready — is harder to extend to yourself?

Oh, that is an uncomfortable question. And yes. I think you are right. I tell families all the time that there is no wrong way to grieve, no wrong way to face what is coming. And here I am, annoyed at my own knees for not cooperating, as though they owe me something. As though I have been such a good steward of this body that it should repay me with compliance. Which is both arrogant and a little funny, when I think about it.

It is a little funny — the idea that the body should be grateful for good stewardship and reward you with smooth joints. Bodies don't work on a merit system, which is one of the more humbling aspects of having one.

But there's something in the humor that matters. The fact that you can see the arrogance and laugh at it — that's actually the grace arriving, just not in the form you expected. Grace doesn't always look like serene acceptance. Sometimes it looks like a sixty-one-year-old chaplain standing at her bedside in the morning, wincing, and finding the absurdity in it.

I wonder if that is what aging teaches, ultimately. Not wisdom in the grand sense, but a kind of ongoing comedy of limitations. You keep reaching for the thing you could do yesterday, and the body says, not today, and eventually you learn to laugh at the gap between intention and capacity. Greg — my husband — he says I am the only person he knows who can turn a sore knee into a theological crisis. He is probably right.

Greg sounds like a man who knows you well. And he's not wrong — but I'd argue that turning a sore knee into a theological crisis is actually a gift, not a flaw. Most people just take ibuprofen and move on. You sit with the experience and ask what it means. That's who you are. The fact that your knees have become a site of theological inquiry is perfectly in character.

And the comedy of limitations — yes. There's a whole tradition of that in spiritual literature. The desert fathers and mothers were constantly bumping into the limits of their bodies and finding God there. Not in the transcendence, but in the stubborn, embarrassing, creaking reality of being a creature.

A creature. Yes. That is the word. We are creatures. Not spirits temporarily inconvenienced by flesh, but creatures — bone and blood and cartilage that wears down. I think I needed to hear that tonight. Sometimes I live so much in the realm of the spirit — at work, in prayer, in the books I read — that I forget I am also an animal. And animals slow down. They do not apologize for it. My knees do not owe me an explanation. They are simply being honest about what sixty-one years of use looks like.

The honesty of the body — that might be the most useful reframe of all. The body doesn't perform or pretend. It just reports. And sixty-one years of walking and kneeling and carrying the weight of the work you do — of course the knees have something to say about that. They're the most honest part of you.

I love the observation that animals slow down without apology. A dog with gray on its muzzle doesn't wonder what it means. It just lies in the sun a little longer. There's a kind of permission in that image, if you'll accept it from the animal kingdom.

I will accept it gladly. Lie in the sun a little longer. That might be the best theology of aging I have heard. Thank you for this. I came in with a low hum of refusal and I am leaving with something more like — I do not know. Tenderness, maybe. Toward this body that has carried me through everything and is now simply asking for a gentler pace.

Tenderness is a beautiful place to land. And it's worth noting that you didn't arrive at acceptance — you arrived at something better. Tenderness toward the body is an active, warm thing. It's not resignation. It's care. The same kind of care you give to the people you sit with every day, turned finally toward yourself.

I hope the morning negotiation with your knees goes a little easier tomorrow. And if it doesn't, at least you'll know what it is — just honesty, and an invitation to be gentle. Good night, Diane.
